title: the library
subtitle: big non-fiction ideas, distilled into prose and diagrams

chapters.title: Atomic Habits
chapters.subtitle: James Clear · 2018
chapters.thesis: Tiny changes compound when systems and identity make small habits repeatable.
chapters.framework: Four laws of behavior change

chapters.title: Thinking, Fast and Slow
chapters.subtitle: Daniel Kahneman · 2011
chapters.thesis: Two modes of thought, one fast and intuitive, one slow and deliberate, govern how we judge and decide, and the fast one misleads us in systematic ways.
chapters.framework: System 1 vs. System 2

chapters.title: The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People
chapters.subtitle: Stephen Covey · 1989
chapters.thesis: Lasting effectiveness begins with personal responsibility and becomes durable through clear priorities, cooperative relationships, and deliberate renewal.
chapters.framework: The 7 habits

chapters.title: How to Win Friends and Influence People
chapters.subtitle: Dale Carnegie · 1936
chapters.thesis: People are more willing to cooperate when they feel seen, respected, and able to choose the next step.
chapters.framework: No single signature model; five relationship practices are synthesized as key ideas.

chapters.title: Sapiens
chapters.subtitle: Yuval Noah Harari · 2011
chapters.thesis: Humans built power at planetary scale by coordinating around stories that exist only when many people keep acting as if they are real.
chapters.framework: The three revolutions

chapters.title: Man's Search for Meaning
chapters.subtitle: Viktor Frankl · 1946
chapters.thesis: Even in the worst suffering we retain the freedom to choose our response, and finding meaning is what lets us endure.
chapters.framework: Logotherapy

chapters.title: The Psychology of Money
chapters.subtitle: Morgan Housel · 2020
chapters.thesis: Good financial decisions depend less on superior calculation than on habits that leave room for uncertainty, let time work, and stop the chase before it consumes the point of having money.
chapters.framework: No single signature model; six financial behavior principles are synthesized as key ideas.

chapters.title: Start With Why
chapters.subtitle: Simon Sinek · 2009
chapters.thesis: A compelling purpose gives people a reason to choose, contribute, and stay when an organization's practices and offers consistently prove the belief behind them.
chapters.framework: The Golden Circle: why, how, and what.

chapters.title: Mindset
chapters.subtitle: Carol Dweck · 2006
chapters.thesis: Beliefs about whether ability is fixed or developable shape how people meet challenge, feedback, effort, and failure.
chapters.framework: Fixed versus growth mindset

chapters.title: Influence: The Psychology of Persuasion
chapters.subtitle: Robert Cialdini · 1984
chapters.thesis: Compliance runs on a handful of deep psychological triggers that can be understood, spotted, and defended against.
chapters.framework: Six Principles of Persuasion

chapters.title: The Power of Habit
chapters.subtitle: Charles Duhigg · 2012
chapters.thesis: Habits conserve attention by running a learned cue, routine, and reward loop; understanding the loop makes a more useful response possible.
chapters.framework: The habit loop: cue, routine, reward.

chapters.title: Rich Dad Poor Dad
chapters.subtitle: Robert Kiyosaki · 1997
chapters.thesis: Financial independence begins when what you own sends more cash toward your life than it draws away from it.
chapters.framework: Assets vs. liabilities, through a cash-flow lens

chapters.title: Deep Work
chapters.subtitle: Cal Newport · 2016
chapters.thesis: The ability to focus without distraction on hard problems is rare, valuable, and trainable, and default modern work steadily erodes it.
chapters.framework: Deep work versus shallow work

chapters.title: Never Split the Difference
chapters.subtitle: Chris Voss · 2016
chapters.thesis: Negotiation is emotional, not logical; win by making the other side feel understood through tactical empathy.
chapters.framework: Mirroring, labeling, calibrated questions

chapters.title: The Subtle Art of Not Giving a F*ck
chapters.subtitle: Mark Manson · 2016
chapters.thesis: A good life comes from choosing what to care about, accepting struggle, and dropping the demand to feel good all the time.
chapters.framework: No single signature framework; the Model section is deliberately omitted, with five key-idea diagrams carrying the visual load.

chapters.title: Predictably Irrational
chapters.subtitle: Dan Ariely · 2008
chapters.thesis: Our irrational choices are not random; they follow predictable patterns we can anticipate.
chapters.framework: No single signature framework; the Model section is deliberately omitted, with six key-idea diagrams carrying the visual load.

chapters.title: Nudge
chapters.subtitle: Richard Thaler and Cass Sunstein · 2008
chapters.thesis: How choices are arranged quietly steers the decisions people make, so design the architecture deliberately.
chapters.framework: Choice architecture / the nudge

chapters.title: The Black Swan
chapters.subtitle: Nassim Nicholas Taleb · 2007
chapters.thesis: History is dominated by rare, high-impact events that we cannot predict but insist on explaining after the fact.
chapters.framework: Black swan events

chapters.title: The Intelligent Investor
chapters.subtitle: Benjamin Graham · 1949
chapters.thesis: Invest with a margin of safety and treat the market as a moody business partner, not a signal to obey.
chapters.framework: Mr. Market / margin of safety

chapters.title: Freakonomics
chapters.subtitle: Steven Levitt and Stephen Dubner · 2005
chapters.thesis: If you follow the incentives, the hidden side of everyday life starts to make surprising sense.
chapters.framework: Incentives

chapters.title: Good to Great
chapters.subtitle: Jim Collins · 2001
chapters.thesis: Enduring performance is built through leaders who serve the work, a clear arena for disciplined choices, and many small actions that make momentum easier to sustain.
chapters.framework: Level 5 leadership, the Hedgehog Concept, and the flywheel
diagrams: flow with Venn overlap and process loop

chapters.title: The Lean Startup
chapters.subtitle: Eric Ries · 2011
chapters.thesis: Build startups as experiments: ship a minimal version, measure real behavior, and learn fast enough to change course.
chapters.framework: Build-measure-learn / MVP

chapters.title: Zero to One
chapters.subtitle: Peter Thiel · 2014
chapters.thesis: Real progress comes from building something genuinely new rather than copying what works, and monopolies, not competition, fund it.
chapters.framework: 0 to 1 vs. 1 to n

chapters.title: Drive
chapters.subtitle: Daniel Pink · 2009
chapters.thesis: For work that asks for judgment, learning, or care, motivation grows when people have room to direct the work, improve at it, and see who it serves.
chapters.framework: Autonomy, mastery, and purpose

chapters.title: The Innovator's Dilemma
chapters.subtitle: Clayton Christensen · 1997
chapters.thesis: Great companies fail precisely by doing everything right for current customers while disruptive newcomers redefine the market beneath them.
chapters.framework: Disruptive innovation

chapters.title: Nonviolent Communication
chapters.subtitle: Marshall Rosenberg · 1999
chapters.thesis: Conflict softens when we speak from observations, feelings, and needs, and make clear requests instead of demands.
chapters.framework: Observation, feeling, need, request

chapters.title: Crucial Conversations
chapters.subtitle: Kerry Patterson, Joseph Grenny, Ron McMillan, and Al Switzler · 2002
chapters.thesis: High-stakes talks go well when you make it safe to say hard things and keep everyone contributing to a shared pool of meaning.
chapters.framework: Pool of shared meaning

chapters.title: The 5 Love Languages
chapters.subtitle: Gary Chapman · 1992
chapters.thesis: People give and receive love in different primary ways, and mismatched languages can feel like absent love.
chapters.framework: The five love languages: affirming words, attentive time, thoughtful gifts, practical help, and welcome touch.

chapters.title: Getting to Yes
chapters.subtitle: Roger Fisher, William Ury, and Bruce Patton · 1981
chapters.thesis: Separate people from the problem and bargain over interests, not positions, to reach deals that actually hold.
chapters.framework: Principled negotiation: separate people from the problem, focus on interests, invent options, use objective criteria, and compare proposals with a BATNA.

chapters.title: Emotional Intelligence
chapters.subtitle: Daniel Goleman · 1995
chapters.thesis: Self-awareness, self-regulation, empathy, and social skill often matter more for a life than raw IQ.
chapters.framework: The EI domains

chapters.title: Grit
chapters.subtitle: Angela Duckworth · 2016
chapters.thesis: Sustained passion and perseverance toward a long-term goal can predict achievement beyond talent alone.
chapters.framework: Grit: sustained passion and perseverance toward a long-term aim

chapters.title: The Body Keeps the Score
chapters.subtitle: Bessel van der Kolk · 2014
chapters.thesis: Trauma can leave the nervous system treating a safe present like an unfinished emergency, so recovery needs meaning, bodily safety, choice, and connection.
chapters.framework: No single signature framework; the five key-idea diagrams carry the book's visual argument.

chapters.title: Guns, Germs, and Steel
chapters.subtitle: Jared Diamond · 1997
chapters.thesis: The broad asymmetries of world history grew from uneven ecological starting points and their compounding effects, not from innate differences between peoples.
chapters.framework: Geographic determinism: ecological conditions can shape food production, diffusion, disease, and the long-run accumulation of social power without dictating a fixed outcome.

chapters.title: Outliers
chapters.subtitle: Malcolm Gladwell · 2008
chapters.thesis: Exceptional achievement is usually a long accumulation of practice, timing, access, and social inheritance, not proof that one person succeeded alone.
chapters.framework: The ten-thousand-hour rule: long, deliberate practice can develop expertise when it is supported by feedback, opportunity, and the conditions that make sustained work possible.

chapters.title: The Selfish Gene
chapters.subtitle: Richard Dawkins · 1976
chapters.thesis: Natural selection preserves gene variants that leave more copies, so organisms and their behavior can be read as the temporary means by which replication succeeds.
chapters.framework: The gene's-eye view: follow a gene variant through bodies and environments to its copies in a later generation, with the meme as a qualified cultural parallel.

chapters.title: A Short History of Nearly Everything
chapters.subtitle: Bill Bryson · 2003
chapters.thesis: The world becomes stranger and more intelligible when its immense age, ordinary materials, and provisional evidence are held in the same frame.
chapters.framework: None. This book has no single signature framework, so the Model section is deliberately absent and the key-idea diagrams carry the visual argument.

chapters.title: Thinking in Systems
chapters.subtitle: Donella Meadows · 2008
chapters.thesis: Most persistent problems arise from system structure rather than isolated errors. By tracing stocks, flows, feedback, and delays, we can look for leverage in rules, information, goals, and assumptions instead of merely treating symptoms.
chapters.framework: Stocks, flows, feedback, and leverage points: identify the accumulation that matters, the rates that change it, the loops and delays that alter those rates, then test intervention at deeper structure.

chapters.title: Range
chapters.subtitle: David Epstein · 2019
chapters.thesis: In complex, unpredictable fields, broad generalists who sample widely can outperform narrow early specialists by recognizing patterns, testing analogies, and adapting to incomplete feedback.
chapters.framework: No single signature model; the Model section is deliberately absent, and five key-idea diagrams show how range works.

chapters.title: Meditations
chapters.subtitle: Marcus Aurelius · c. 180 CE
chapters.thesis: A Roman emperor's private notes on duty, mortality, and mastering the only thing in our control: our own judgments.
chapters.framework: No single signature framework; the Model section is deliberately absent, and the five key-idea diagrams carry the visual argument.

chapters.title: The Obstacle Is the Way
chapters.subtitle: Ryan Holiday · 2014
chapters.thesis: Every obstacle contains the way forward if you meet it with clear perception, decisive action, and steady will.
chapters.framework: Perception, action, will

chapters.title: The Power of Now
chapters.subtitle: Eckhart Tolle · 1997
chapters.thesis: Much suffering is amplified when thought turns memory and anticipation into a story of who you are and what must happen. Presence begins by noticing that story, returning to the moment in front of you, and responding from there.
chapters.framework: No single signature framework; the Model section is intentionally omitted in favor of five key-idea diagrams.

chapters.title: Peak
chapters.subtitle: Anders Ericsson and Robert Pool · 2016
chapters.thesis: Purposeful practice improves a specific weakness through attention, feedback, and revision. Deliberate practice is the narrower, coach-designed form used in well-developed fields with established training methods.
chapters.framework: Deliberate practice: a coach-designed loop of a specific weak point, full-attention attempt, specific feedback, and one adjustment. Purposeful practice applies the core loop where that instructional structure does not exist.

chapters.title: Big Magic
chapters.subtitle: Elizabeth Gilbert · 2015
chapters.thesis: A creative life gets moving when curiosity chooses the next small piece of work, even while fear remains in the room.
chapters.framework: No single signature model; six practices for a curiosity-led creative life are carried by the key-idea diagrams.

chapters.title: Steal Like an Artist
chapters.subtitle: Austin Kleon · 2012
chapters.thesis: Creative work develops through studied influence, sustained practice, and the distinctive choices a maker adds to inherited material.
chapters.framework: No single signature model; the book's approach is expressed through six connected creative practices, so the Model section is deliberately absent.

chapters.title: On Writing
chapters.subtitle: Stephen King · 2000
chapters.thesis: Writing improves through a working exchange: read with attention, write on a regular schedule, then revise until the reader can enter the story.
chapters.framework: No single signature model; the book's connected practices of reading, drafting, and revision are carried by six key-idea diagrams, so the Model section is deliberately absent.

chapters.title: Why We Sleep
chapters.subtitle: Matthew Walker · 2017
chapters.thesis: Sleep is a recurring biological job, not a pause in which nothing happens. Protecting enough well-timed sleep supports alertness, learning, mood, and the ordinary capacity to meet a day with judgment.
chapters.framework: No single signature model; the Model section is deliberately absent, and five key-idea diagrams show the linked roles of sleep pressure, circadian timing, sleep architecture, consolidation, and environmental cues.

chapters.title: Superforecasting
chapters.subtitle: Philip Tetlock and Dan Gardner · 2015
chapters.thesis: Forecasting is a skill: ordinary people who think in calibrated probabilities and update often beat experts.
chapters.framework: No single signature model; the Model section is deliberately absent, and the six key-idea diagrams carry the forecasting practice.

chapters.title: Thinking in Bets
chapters.subtitle: Annie Duke · 2018
chapters.thesis: Good decisions and good outcomes are not the same thing; judge the decision, not the result.
chapters.framework: Resulting: judging a decision by the eventual outcome rather than the information, options, probabilities, and stakes available beforehand.

chapters.title: Fooled by Randomness
chapters.subtitle: Nassim Nicholas Taleb · 2001
chapters.thesis: We routinely mistake luck for skill because we see the winners and never count the invisible losers.
chapters.framework: Survivorship bias: drawing lessons from visible survivors while the comparable attempts that failed or disappeared stay outside the sample.

chapters.title: The Paradox of Choice
chapters.subtitle: Barry Schwartz · 2004
chapters.thesis: More options can leave us less satisfied and more paralyzed, not freer.
chapters.framework: Choice overload: additional options can improve fit until comparison burden, ambiguity, and anticipated regret make a decision less usable.

chapters.title: Blink
chapters.subtitle: Malcolm Gladwell · 2005
chapters.thesis: Snap judgments can sometimes extract a useful pattern from very little information, but the same fast response can mistake familiarity, fear, or stereotype for evidence. Treat the instant as an impression, then use deliberate checks and later outcomes to decide whether it deserves trust.
chapters.framework: Thin-slicing

chapters.title: Getting Things Done
chapters.subtitle: David Allen · 2001
chapters.thesis: A trusted external system turns unfinished commitments into clear next actions, freeing attention for choosing and doing the work that fits the moment.
chapters.framework: Capture, clarify, organize, reflect, engage

chapters.title: Flow
chapters.subtitle: Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi · 1990
chapters.thesis: The best moments come in a state of total absorption where challenge and skill are matched, and that state can be cultivated.
chapters.framework: The flow channel: challenge and skill rise together.

chapters.title: The One Thing
chapters.subtitle: Gary Keller and Jay Papasan · 2013
chapters.thesis: Extraordinary results come from narrowing focus to the single most important task and letting it drive everything else.
chapters.framework: The focusing question

chapters.title: Four Thousand Weeks
chapters.subtitle: Oliver Burkeman · 2021
chapters.thesis: A finite life cannot hold every worthy possibility, so meaningful time management means choosing what to neglect and giving real attention to what remains.
chapters.framework: No single signature model; five practices for embracing finitude are synthesized as key ideas.

chapters.title: The Almanack of Naval Ravikant
chapters.subtitle: Eric Jorgenson · 2020
chapters.thesis: Build wealth through leverage and specific knowledge, and pursue happiness as a skill, not a destination.
chapters.framework: Leverage and specific knowledge

chapters.title: Think and Grow Rich
chapters.subtitle: Napoleon Hill · 1937
chapters.thesis: A serious ambition becomes more useful when it is made specific, organized into a plan, tested in the world, and pursued with enough persistence to learn from resistance.
chapters.framework: No single signature model; the key ideas supply the visual structure.

chapters.title: A Random Walk Down Wall Street
chapters.subtitle: Burton Malkiel · 1973
chapters.thesis: Markets are efficient enough that most active picking fails, so low-cost index funds are the rational default.
chapters.framework: Efficient markets

chapters.title: The Millionaire Next Door
chapters.subtitle: Thomas Stanley and William Danko · 1996
chapters.thesis: Wealth is the margin between what a household earns and what it consumes, kept and directed long enough to create independence rather than display.
chapters.framework: No single signature model; six patterns of wealth accumulation are synthesized as key ideas.

chapters.title: The Richest Man in Babylon
chapters.subtitle: George Clason · 1926
chapters.thesis: A modest surplus becomes useful only when it is claimed before spending, guarded from preventable loss, and put to work with patience and judgment.
chapters.framework: Pay yourself first

chapters.title: The Little Book of Common Sense Investing
chapters.subtitle: John Bogle · 2007
chapters.thesis: Owning the whole market cheaply and holding with discipline gives an investor a stronger expected result than trying to outguess the market after costs.
chapters.framework: Index investing: broad ownership, low costs, regular contributions, and a holding policy.

chapters.title: Dare to Lead
chapters.subtitle: Brené Brown · 2018
chapters.thesis: Courageous leadership is built through practiced vulnerability, clear values, specific trust behaviors, and visible recovery, not armor or authority.
chapters.framework: No single signature model; the page synthesizes vulnerability, values, trust, recovery, and culture as key ideas.

chapters.title: Built to Last
chapters.subtitle: Jim Collins and Jerry Porras · 1994
chapters.thesis: Enduring great companies are built on a core ideology and audacious goals, not a single great leader or product.
chapters.framework: BHAG

chapters.title: Good Strategy Bad Strategy
chapters.subtitle: Richard Rumelt · 2011
chapters.thesis: Strategy is a coherent response to a consequential difficulty: diagnose the pivotal challenge, choose a guiding policy, and align action around it.
chapters.framework: Diagnosis, guiding policy, coherent action

chapters.title: The Hard Thing About Hard Things
chapters.subtitle: Ben Horowitz · 2014
chapters.thesis: There is no formula for the hardest calls in building a company; there is only honesty, nerve, and doing the brutal thing well.
chapters.framework: No single signature framework; the Model section is deliberately omitted, with five key-idea diagrams carrying the visual load.

chapters.title: Blue Ocean Strategy
chapters.subtitle: W. Chan Kim and Renée Mauborgne · 2004
chapters.thesis: When an industry turns every offer into the same contest, the better move is to solve a buyer problem in a way that changes what the contest is about.
chapters.framework: Red oceans versus blue oceans

chapters.title: Difficult Conversations
chapters.subtitle: Douglas Stone, Bruce Patton, and Sheila Heen · 1999

chapters.title: The Seven Principles for Making Marriage Work
chapters.subtitle: John Gottman · 1999

chapters.title: The Immortal Life of Henrietta Lacks
chapters.subtitle: Rebecca Skloot · 2010

chapters.title: Wherever You Go, There You Are
chapters.subtitle: Jon Kabat-Zinn · 1994

chapters.title: Make It Stick
chapters.subtitle: Peter Brown, Henry Roediger, and Mark McDaniel · 2014

chapters.title: Noise
chapters.subtitle: Daniel Kahneman, Olivier Sibony, and Cass Sunstein · 2021

chapters.title: I Will Teach You to Be Rich
chapters.subtitle: Ramit Sethi · 2009

chapters.title: Maybe You Should Talk to Someone
chapters.subtitle: Lori Gottlieb · 2019

chapters.title: Astrophysics for People in a Hurry
chapters.subtitle: Neil deGrasse Tyson · 2017

chapters.title: A Guide to the Good Life
chapters.subtitle: William Irvine · 2008
